summary: Judah Halevi (c. 1075-1141), of Muslim and then Christian Spain, is generally regarded as the greatest Hebrew poet of the Andalusian golden age, author of secular and liturgical verse including the Songs of Zion, and of the Kuzari (Kitab al-Khazari), a philosophical dialogue in which the king of the Khazars, seeking the true religion, questions a philosopher, a Christian, a Muslim, and finally a rabbi, and converts to Judaism. The work defends Judaism on the ground of the historical experience of revelation against Aristotelian philosophy.
description:
Judah Halevi (Hebrew Yehudah ha-Levi, in Arabic Abu al-Hasan al-Lawi) was born in Spain, in the era when the Iberian Jewish communities lived under Muslim rule in al-Andalus and, increasingly, under the advancing Christian kingdoms of the north. He is generally regarded in the scholarship as the greatest Hebrew poet of the Andalusian golden age, master of both the secular genres of the Arabic-influenced Hebrew poetry of Spain (poems of friendship, love, and wine) and the liturgical and devotional forms, and above all of the Songs of Zion (Shirei Tziyon), the poems of longing for the Land of Israel of which 'My heart is in the East' (Libi ba-mizrah) is the most celebrated. He worked as a physician and moved between the Muslim south and the Christian north of the peninsula.

His major prose work is the Kuzari, in Arabic Kitab al-Khazari, more fully titled the Book of Refutation and Proof on Behalf of the Despised Religion (Kitab al-radd wa-l-dalil fi al-din al-dhalil). It is a philosophical dialogue framed by a historical premise: the king of the Khazars, a people reported to have converted to Judaism, is troubled by a recurring dream in which, as the text has it, 'an angel addressed him, saying: Thy way of thinking is indeed pleasing to the Creator, but not thy way of acting' (Kuzari 1:1, Hirschfeld translation, read on Sefaria). Seeking the way of acting that will please God, the king questions in turn a philosopher, a Christian, and a Muslim, and, unsatisfied, resolves as the text puts it to 'ask the Jews', finally summoning a rabbi. In the work's turning point the rabbi opens not with metaphysical proofs but with the historical experience of the people of Israel: 'I believe in the God of Abraham, Isaac and Israel, who led the children of Israel out of Egypt with signs and miracles' (Kuzari 1:17, read line-by-line on Sefaria). The dialogue that follows develops Halevi's attributed argument that the ground of religious certainty is not the God of the philosophers reached by speculation but the God of Abraham known through the shared historical experience of revelation, an argument that the scholarship reads as a critique of the Aristotelian philosophy then ascendant among Jewish and Muslim thinkers. Within the dialogue Halevi's rabbi also characterizes the philosopher, the Christian, and the Muslim and argues the case for Judaism; these characterizations and the conversion of the king are the literary and apologetic framing of Halevi's own work, presented here as such and not as this entry's account of Christianity, Islam, or philosophy, on which the entry does not rule.

Yochanan Silman's study reads the Kuzari against the development of Halevi's thought, distinguishing strata that belong to different periods of his intellectual life, and Barry Kogan's scholarship treats its argument and its Arabic philosophical setting; Diana Lobel's study reads the work's vocabulary of religious experience against its Sufi and Islamic philosophical background, showing how Halevi infused Arabic terms for the encounter with the divine with a Jewish theological meaning. Late in life Halevi set out from Spain for the Land of Israel, the goal of his Songs of Zion; he reached Egypt, where the documents of the Cairo Geniza attest his stay, and the traditional accounts hold that he died not long after, around 1141, on or near the completion of the journey. He is presented here on Judaism's own terms as poet and thinker, with the philosophical and literary scholarship recorded alongside as attributed sources.
editorial_notes: This entry presents Judah Halevi on Judaism's own terms as the foremost Hebrew poet of the Andalusian golden age and as the author of the Kuzari (neutrality #1, #4, #6). NEUTRALITY #11 CRUX (the Kuzari's treatment of other faiths): the Kuzari is a dialogue in which Halevi's rabbi characterizes a philosopher, a Christian, and a Muslim and argues the case for Judaism, and in which the Khazar king converts. This entry presents all of that strictly as HALEVI'S OWN attributed apologetic position and literary framing, explicitly flagged in the description as 'the literary and apologetic framing of Halevi's own work ... not as this entry's account of Christianity, Islam, or philosophy, on which the entry does not rule.' The entry does NOT adopt, endorse, or repeat as fact any characterization of Christianity, Islam, or philosophy that the Kuzari makes, does not reproduce the apologetic comparisons themselves, and does not adjudicate among the faiths; it records only that Halevi's work argues for Judaism, attributed to Halevi (neutrality #1-#2, #10-#11). Halevi's critique of Aristotelian philosophy is likewise given as his attributed position, not ruled on. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: (a) academic historians of the Kuzari and of Halevi's philosophy (Silman; Kogan); (b) the primary text, open and auditable on Sefaria (the Kuzari in the public-domain Hirschfeld 1905 English translation; 1:1 and 1:17 read line-by-line, with the angel's words and the rabbi's opening statement quoted verbatim); and (c) a contemporary Jewish scholar (Diana Lobel; Silman and Kogan also carry this lens). No encyclopedias (the Encyclopaedia Judaica, the Jewish Encyclopedia, Chabad.org, My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. Confidence: the Kuzari on Sefaria is rated high because 1:1 and 1:17 were read line-by-line for this entry, with the angel's words ('Thy way of thinking is indeed pleasing to the Creator, but not thy way of acting') and the rabbi's opening ('I believe in the God of Abraham, Isaac and Israel, who led the children of Israel out of Egypt with signs and miracles') quoted verbatim from the Hirschfeld translation; the secondary monographs (Silman, Lobel) are medium, verified via publisher and library records, not read line-by-line. FLAG: the Kogan/Berman English Kuzari translation for the Yale Judaica Series has been long in preparation and its publication status is uncertain; it is cited cautiously as a scholarly project with Kogan's Halevi scholarship, rated medium, and the load-bearing primary quotations rest on the Hirschfeld translation actually read on Sefaria, not on Kogan. Relation targets rabbinic-judaism, torah, tanakh, and maimonides exist; kuzari (the work) is a dangling forward reference (authored elsewhere in this batch, dangling ok, flagged).

lifespan.note: c. 1075-1141 CE; born in Spain (Tudela or Toledo are both reported in the scholarship), died c. 1141 after setting out for the Land of Israel and reaching Egypt, as attested in the Cairo Geniza documents. Birth year approximate.

relations.note: Halevi wrote from within the rabbinic tradition of medieval Sephardic Judaism, and the Kuzari's rabbi speaks for that tradition. The rabbinic-judaism entry exists.

Halevi is the author of the Kuzari (Kitab al-Khazari), the philosophical dialogue defending Judaism on the ground of the historical experience of revelation. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created work entity, authored elsewhere in this batch (flagged).

The Kuzari develops Halevi's attributed critique of the Aristotelian philosophy then ascendant among Jewish and Muslim thinkers, opposing the God of Abraham known through historical experience to the God of the philosophers reached by speculation. Recorded as Halevi's attributed position, not adjudicated. The aristotle entry exists (philosophy).
Halevi's Songs of Zion and the Kuzari draw throughout on the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh), and the rabbi's argument rests on the biblical history of Israel. The tanakh entry exists.
The Kuzari's defense of Judaism turns on the revelation of the Torah and the historical experience of Israel at Sinai and the Exodus. The torah entry exists.

Halevi (c. 1075-1141) belongs to the generation just before Maimonides (1138-1204); both are central figures of medieval Sephardic Jewish thought, though the Kuzari's stance toward philosophy differs markedly from Maimonides' Aristotelian program. Recorded as a contrast in intellectual orientation, not a ranking. The maimonides entry exists.

sources.citation: The Kuzari (Kitab al-Khazari), open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria (Kitab al Khazari, translated by Hartwig Hirschfeld, 1905). Cited as the primary text for the frame of the dialogue and its turning point: the king's recurring dream in which 'an angel addressed him, saying: Thy way of thinking is indeed pleasing to the Creator, but not thy way of acting' (Kuzari 1:1), and the rabbi's opening statement 'I believe in the God of Abraham, Isaac and Israel, who led the children of Israel out of Egypt with signs and miracles' (Kuzari 1:17). Kuzari 1:1 and 1:17 read line-by-line and quoted verbatim from the Hirschfeld translation on Sefaria. The dialogue's characterizations of the philosopher, the Christian, and the Muslim and its argument for Judaism are Halevi's own attributed apologetic and literary framing, recorded as such and not adopted or adjudicated by this entry (neutrality #11).
sources.license: Arabic original and Hebrew (Ibn Tibbon) translation in the public domain; the Hirschfeld 1905 English translation in the public domain. Kuzari 1:1 and 1:17 read line-by-line via the Sefaria text API.

sources.citation: Silman, Yochanan. Philosopher and Prophet: Judah Halevi, the Kuzari, and the Evolution of His Thought. Translated by Lenn J. Schramm. SUNY Series in Judaica. Albany: State University of New York Press, 1995 (ISBN 0791424626). A scholarly study relating the strata of the Kuzari to distinct periods in the development of Halevi's thought and to its systematic difficulties; cited as an academic lens on the Kuzari's argument and composition. Verified to exist and be on-topic (SUNY Press record), not read line-by-line.

Lobel, Diana. Between Mysticism and Philosophy: Sufi Language of Religious Experience in Judah Ha-Levi's Kuzari. SUNY Series in Jewish Philosophy. Albany: State University of New York Press, 2000 (ISBN 079144452X). A contemporary Jewish scholar's study reading the Kuzari's vocabulary of religious experience against its Sufi and Islamic philosophical background, showing how Halevi gave Arabic terms for the encounter with the divine a Jewish theological meaning; cited as the contemporary scholarly lens and for the Kuzari's Islamic philosophical setting (neutrality #10-#11, as scholarship on Halevi's use of a shared vocabulary, not as a claim about Islam). Verified to exist and be on-topic (SUNY Press record; reviewed in AJS Review and JAOS), not read line-by-line.

Kogan, Barry S. The scholarship of Barry S. Kogan on Judah Halevi and the Kuzari, including the English translation of the Kuzari (The Book of the Kuzari) begun by the late Lawrence V. Berman and prepared for the Yale Judaica Series, Yale University Press. Cited as an academic lens on the Kuzari's argument and its Arabic philosophical setting. NOTE / FLAG: the Kogan-Berman Yale Judaica translation has been long in preparation and its publication status is uncertain; it is cited cautiously as a scholarly project, and the load-bearing primary quotations in this entry rest on the public-domain Hirschfeld 1905 translation actually read on Sefaria, not on Kogan. Verified that Kogan is a recognized Halevi scholar and that the Yale Judaica project exists.
